sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Raised five children at his cousin-married parents' homestead
narrative_blocks.body: William Jr. inherited and raised his five children at the Dover Furnace homestead his physician father had established after leaving Berlin Connecticut. He and Sarah Varney Cutler stayed in Dover their entire married life. He is buried at South Dover Rural Cemetery beside both of his cousin-married parents.

narrative_blocks.header: A charter member, twice, in Pawling
narrative_blocks.body: Effie was a charter member of both the Pawling Grange and the Pawling Baptist Church — a founding presence in the town’s civic and religious life.

parents.father_research_notes: Oscar Augustus Dykeman of Pawling NY. Per EH 1909: 'son of Oscar Augustus and Louisa (Denton) Dykeman of Pawling NY.'

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Farmed in Wingdale Dutchess County NY area. He and Effie took in boarders — typically developmentally disabled individuals for whom the state paid room and board. Active in Pawling Baptist Church; name appears on one of the church's stained glass windows as a major contributor.

narrative_blocks.header: Farmer and Pawling Baptist church pillar
narrative_blocks.body: Peter and Effie Dykeman ran a farm in Wingdale and opened their home to state-supported boarders — developmentally disabled individuals for whom the state paid their keep. Peter was a major contributor to the Pawling Baptist Church, his name inscribed on one of the windows.

narrative_blocks.header: Boarders the state sent to the farm
narrative_blocks.body: Peter and Effie took in boarders at their Pawling farm — usually developmentally disabled people whom the state paid the family to care for.

narrative_blocks.header: Polio came; her parents bought a pony
narrative_blocks.body: Nellie contracted polio as a child in Pawling. Her parents bought her a pony, and she got from place to place riding it.

bio.bio_blurb: Founded the four-generation Dykeman Family Farm

career.title: Farmer and roadside market founder
career.organization: Henry Dykeman roadside market, Pawling NY
career.notes: Co-founded the Henry Dykeman roadside market in Pawling NY.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded a farm that outlived him
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Dykeman and his wife Velma founded the Dykeman Family Farm, a vegetable operation that grew into a four-generation business still running into the 2020s. He carried on the family’s tradition of annual reunions begun by his parents.

narrative_blocks.header: A Model-A turned camper, newlyweds, California
narrative_blocks.body: In their first year of marriage, Henry and Velma converted a Model A into a makeshift camper and drove it from New York to California and back. They stayed married for sixty years.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father William Hooker Jr. (H02791) — third consecutive William in three generations: H01429 Dr. William, H02791 William Jr., H04403 William III.

narrative_blocks.body: William the Third extended the Dr. William naming line into a third generation in Dover. His grandfather Dr. William Hooker had migrated from Berlin Connecticut to South Dover and served in the New York legislature; his father William Jr. was born and died at Dover Furnace.

documents.document_blurb: 1838 letter from former Dover neighbor Mott Titus describing his Ohio migration, the canal boom, frontier economics, and the Whig sweep of New York's 1838 elections.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Practiced medicine in South Dover Dutchess County NY for decades.

career.title: State Legislator
career.notes: Represented the town of Dover in the NY State Legislature; described as a prominent citizen.

narrative_blocks.header: South Dover physician served NY legislature
narrative_blocks.body: William left Berlin Connecticut for Dutchess County New York where he settled as a physician at South Dover. He became a prominent citizen and represented the town of Dover in the New York State Legislature. He died at Dover Furnace in March 1853 at age sixty-five.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia carried Hooker blood from both parents — her mother Ann Hooker Stanley descended from Samuel Hooker Jr., her father Seth Stanley descended through his mother Hannah Hart from Abigail Hooker. When she married her cousin Dr. William Hooker in Hartford on January 9, 1814, three Hooker branches converged in their two children — a triple descent that closed a century-and-a-half family loop.

classification.lineage_note: H00533 William (gen 5) → H00265 Seth (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

compiler_connection.notes: William Hooker is the compiler's direct paternal ancestor through the Hooker surname line. His father Seth (H00265) died when William was 2 years old. William is the link between the confirmed paternal line and the compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker. Research needed to trace descendants.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: H00533 William → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker (compiler)

open_questions.question: Full descent from William Hooker (H00533) to Michael Gay Hooker
open_questions.notes: William born 1756 Kensington CT; died 1826. His starred children H01427-H01433 are the next research step. The compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker's connection runs through one of these children.
open_questions.action: Research EH entries 1427-1433; check CT vital records and census records for William's children

primary_residence.notes: Born and died Kensington CT. Also had family connection to Hinsdale NH through wife.

military_service.unit: Capt. Caleb Bull's Company in Webb's Regiment, Continental Army
military_service.notes: Enlisted Apr 21, 1777 as Private for 3 years in Capt. Caleb Bull's Company, Webb's Regiment. Promoted Sergeant May 16, 1780 for an additional 8 months. Member of the Hartford 'band of music' (eight musicians) raised 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line. Pensioned Nov 4, 1818. DAR Ancestor #A057709.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford band of music attached to Connecticut line
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was a member of the eight-man 'band of music' raised in Hartford in 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line of the Continental Army — one of the rare documented Revolutionary War regimental musical ensembles. The band provided drums, fifes, and field music during the war's middle years. The arrangement was unusual: most Revolutionary regiments had ad hoc field musicians rather than a formally organized eight-piece band.

narrative_blocks.header: Three years Private, eight months Sergeant 1777-1780
narrative_blocks.body: William enlisted April 21, 1777 as a Private in Captain Caleb Bull's Company of Webb's Regiment for a three-year term. On May 16, 1780 he was promoted to Sergeant for an additional eight months of service. He was pensioned November 4, 1818 (DAR Ancestor #A057709) — the federal pension secured nearly four decades after his service ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children with Sarah Wilcox of Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: William married Sarah Wilcox of Berlin Connecticut and they had nine documented children together. The family remained in Kensington, the original Berlin Connecticut parish where William had been born in 1756 and where he would die in 1826. The nine-child household was characteristic of the post-Revolutionary Connecticut farming generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Kensington Berlin CT 1756 to 1826 age sixty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was born September 15, 1756 in Kensington (now Berlin) Connecticut and died February 4, 1826 in Kensington at age sixty-nine. He is buried at West Lane Cemetery in Berlin Connecticut. His Revolutionary War service spanned the war's middle period from the bleakest 1777 Valley Forge winter through the war's final 1780-1781 phase.

sources.short: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution, 1889
Berlin-Peck: Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin

cross_connections.display_label: His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.
commanded the company he guarded Boston with in early 1776

military_service.unit: Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regt. (Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Co.); Gen. Wadsworth's Brigade; Maj. Sheldon's Light Horse (Capt. Daniel Sloper's Co.)
military_service.notes: 1776 campaign across three commands. Winter 1776: guarded besieged Boston about six weeks in Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Company, Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regiment. Summer 1776: present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27) with Gen. James Wadsworth's Brigade and the retreat from New York. Nov 4–Dec 24, 1776: Corporal in Capt. Daniel Sloper's Company, Maj. Sheldon's Regiment of Light Horse, on Washington's retreat through New Jersey. Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), pp. 381, 392, 475; Berlin-Peck, Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times, his last two wives widows
narrative_blocks.body: After Ann Hooker died in 1808 he married twice more, both times to widows: Sabra Winchell Stanley, and in 1822 Abigail Hart Smith, herself already twice-widowed. His will, presented in court July 1827, left the farm and stock to his son Seth and the remainder to his daughters Lydia, Nancy, and Amelia.

narrative_blocks.header: His whole war fit in one brutal year
narrative_blocks.body: Seth's Revolutionary service was crowded into 1776. That year he helped guard besieged Boston through the winter, stood with the army in its rout at the Battle of Long Island, and rode in the light horse covering Washington's retreat across New Jersey.

cross_connections.display_label: His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.

burialCemetery.notes: Resting place of the South Dover Hooker family across multiple generations: Dr. William Hooker (H01429, 1788-1853) and his cousin Amelia Stanley Hooker (HD0539, 1790-1885), their two children William Jr. (H02791) and Ann Hooker Vincent (H02792), William Jr.'s wife Sarah Varney Cutler Hooker (X01229) and four of their five children, plus Ann's husband Edwin Vincent (X02738) and her three Vincent children — Ella, Amelia H., Charles William — and onward through the Vincent line via Charles Kenneth and his wife Edith Akin Vincent down to Walter Akin Vincent (HD3593, WWII Navy). The 1814 cousin marriage united two Hooker branches via Amelia's TRIPLE Hooker descent.
